title: Photorealistic Text-to-Image Diffusion Models with Deep Language Understanding

paragraphs.text:
## **Photorealistic Text-to-Image Diffusion Models with Deep Language Understanding**

**Chitwan Saharia** _[∗]_ **, William Chan** _[∗]_ **, Saurabh Saxena** _[†]_ **, Lala Li** _[†]_ **, Jay Whang** _[†]_ **, Emily Denton, Seyed Kamyar Seyed Ghasemipour, Burcu Karagol Ayan, S. Sara Mahdavi, Raphael Gontijo-Lopes, Tim Salimans, Jonathan Ho** _[†]_ **, David J Fleet** _[†‡]_ **, Mohammad Norouzi** _[∗]_

```
{sahariac,williamchan,mnorouzi}@google.com
{srbs,lala,jwhang,jonathanho,davidfleet}@google.com
```

Google Research, Brain Team Toronto, Ontario, Canada

## **Abstract**

We present Imagen, a text-to-image diffusion model with an unprecedented degree of photorealism and a deep level of language understanding. Imagen builds on the power of large transformer language models in understanding text and hinges on the strength of diffusion models in high-fidelity image generation. Our key discovery is that generic large language models (e.g. T5), pretrained on text-only corpora, are surprisingly effective at encoding text for image synthesis: increasing the size of the language model in Imagen boosts both sample fidelity and image-text alignment much more than increasing the size of the image diffusion model. Imagen achieves a new state-of-the-art FID score of 7.27 on the COCO dataset, without ever training on COCO, and human raters find Imagen samples to be on par with the COCO data itself in image-text alignment. To assess text-to-image models in greater depth, we introduce DrawBench, a comprehensive and challenging benchmark for text-to-image models. With DrawBench, we compare Imagen with recent methods including VQ-GAN+CLIP, Latent Diffusion Models, GLIDE and DALL-E 2, and find that human raters prefer Imagen over other models in side-by-side comparisons, both in terms of sample quality and image-text alignment.

## **1 Introduction**

Multimodal learning has come into prominence recently, with text-to-image synthesis [55, 12, 59] and image-text contrastive learning [51, 32, 77] at the forefront. These models have transformed the research community and captured widespread public attention with creative image generation [22, 56] and editing applications [21, 43, 36]. To pursue this research direction further, we introduce Imagen, a text-to-image diffusion model that combines the power of transformer language models (LMs) [15, 54] with high-fidelity diffusion models [28, 29, 16, 43] to deliver an unprecedented degree of photorealism and a deep level of language understanding in text-to-image synthesis. In contrast to prior work that uses only image-text data for model training [e.g., 55, 43], the key finding behind Imagen is that text embeddings from large LMs [54, 15], pretrained on text-only corpora, are remarkably effective for text-to-image synthesis. See Fig. 1 for select samples.
Imagen comprises a frozen T5-XXL [54] encoder to map input text into a sequence of embeddings and a 64 _×_ 64 image diffusion model, followed by two super-resolution diffusion models for generating

2

256 _×_ 256 and 1024 _×_ 1024 images (see Fig. A.4). All diffusion models are conditioned on the text embedding sequence and use classifier-free guidance [27]. Imagen relies on new sampling techniques to allow usage of large guidance weights without sample quality degradation observed in prior work, resulting in images with higher fidelity and better image-text alignment than previously possible.

While conceptually simple and easy to train, Imagen yields surprisingly strong results. Imagen outperforms other methods on COCO [38] with zero-shot FID-30K of 7.27, significantly outperforming prior work such as GLIDE [43] (at 12.4) and the concurrent work of DALL-E 2 [56] (at 10.4). Our zero-shot FID score is also better than state-of-the-art models trained on COCO, e.g., Make-A-Scene [22] (at 7.6). Additionally, human raters indicate that generated samples from Imagen are on-par in image-text alignment to the reference images on COCO captions.

We introduce DrawBench, a new structured suite of text prompts for text-to-image evaluation. DrawBench enables deeper insights through a multi-dimensional evaluation of text-to-image models, with text prompts designed to probe different semantic properties of models. These include compositionality, cardinality, spatial relations, the ability to handle complex text prompts or prompts with rare words, and they include creative prompts that push the limits of models’ ability to generate highly implausible scenes well beyond the scope of the training data. With DrawBench, extensive human evaluation shows that Imagen outperforms other recent methods [59, 12, 56] by a significant margin. We further demonstrate some of the clear advantages of the use of large pre-trained language models [54] over multi-modal embeddings such as CLIP [51] as a text encoder for Imagen.

Key contributions of the paper include:

1. We discover that large frozen language models trained only on text data are surprisingly very effective text encoders for text-to-image generation, and that scaling the size of frozen text encoder improves sample quality significantly more than scaling the size of image diffusion model.
2. We introduce _dynamic thresholding_ , a new diffusion sampling technique to leverage high guidance weights and generating more photorealistic and detailed images than previously possible.
3. We highlight several important diffusion architecture design choices and propose _Efficient U-Net_ , a new architecture variant which is simpler, converges faster and is more memory efficient.
4. We achieve a new state-of-the-art COCO FID of 7.27. Human raters find Imagen to be on-par with the reference images in terms of image-text alignment.

5. We introduce DrawBench, a new comprehensive and challenging evaluation benchmark for the text-to-image task. On DrawBench human evaluation, we find Imagen to outperform all other work, including the concurrent work of DALL-E 2 [56].

## **2 Imagen**

Imagen consists of a text encoder that maps text to a sequence of embeddings and a cascade of conditional diffusion models that map these embeddings to images of increasing resolutions (see Fig. A.4). In the following subsections, we describe each of these components in detail.

## **2.1 Pretrained text encoders**

Text-to-image models need powerful semantic text encoders to capture the complexity and compositionality of arbitrary natural language text inputs. Text encoders trained on paired image-text data are standard in current text-to-image models; they can be trained from scratch [43, 55] or pretrained on image-text data [56] (e.g., CLIP [51]). The image-text training objectives suggest that these text encoders may encode visually semantic and meaningful representations especially relevant for the text-to-image generation task. Large language models can be another models of choice to encode text for text-to-image generation. Recent progress in large language models (e.g., BERT [15], GPT [49, 50, 7], T5 [54]) have led to leaps in textual understanding and generative capabilities. Language models are trained on text only corpus significantly larger than paired image-text data, thus being exposed to a very rich and wide distribution of text. These models are also generally much larger than text encoders in current image-text models [51, 32, 83] (e.g. PaLM [11] has 540B parameters, while CoCa [83] has a _≈_ 1B parameter text encoder).

It thus becomes natural to explore both families of text encoders for the text-to-image task. Imagen explores pretrained text encoders: BERT [15], T5 [53] and CLIP [48]. For simplicity, we freeze the weights of these text encoders. Freezing has several advantages such as offline computation of embeddings, resulting in negligible computation or memory footprint during training of the textto-image model. In our work, we find that there is a clear conviction that scaling the text encoder size improves the quality of text-to-image generation. We also find that while T5-XXL and CLIP

3

text encoders perform similarly on simple benchmarks such as MS-COCO, human evaluators prefer T5-XXL encoders over CLIP text encoders in both image-text alignment and image fidelity on DrawBench, a set of challenging and compositional prompts. We refer the reader to Section 4.4 for summary of our findings, and Appendix D.1 for detailed ablations.

## **2.2 Diffusion models and classifier-free guidance**

Here we give a brief introduction to diffusion models; a precise description is in Appendix A. Diffusion models [66, 28, 68] are a class of generative models that convert Gaussian noise into samples from a learned data distribution via an iterative denoising process. These models can be conditional, for example on class labels, text, or low-resolution images [e.g. 16, 29, 62, 61, 78, 43, 56]. ## **2.3 Large guidance weight samplers**

We corroborate the results of recent text-guided diffusion work [16, 43, 56] and find that increasing the classifier-free guidance weight improves image-text alignment, but damages image fidelity producing highly saturated and unnatural images [27]. We find that this is due to a train-test mismatch arising from high guidance weights. At each sampling step _t_ , the **x** -prediction **x** ˆ _[t]_ 0[must be within the same] bounds as training data **x** , i.e. within [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1], but we find empirically that high guidance weights cause **x** -predictions to exceed these bounds. This is a train-test mismatch, and since the diffusion model is iteratively applied on its own output throughout sampling, the sampling process produces unnatural images and sometimes even diverges. To counter this problem, we investigate _static thresholding_ and _dynamic thresholding_ . See Appendix Fig. A.31 for reference implementation of the techniques and Appendix Fig. A.9 for visualizations of their effects.

**Static thresholding** : We refer to elementwise clipping the **x** -prediction to [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1] as _static thresholding_ . This method was in fact used but not emphasized in previous work [28], and to our knowledge its importance has not been investigated in the context of guided sampling. We discover that static thresholding is essential to sampling with large guidance weights and prevents generation of blank images. Nonetheless, static thresholding still results in over-saturated and less detailed images as the guidance weight further increases.

**Dynamic thresholding** : We introduce a new _dynamic thresholding_ method: at each sampling step we set _s_ to a certain percentile absolute pixel value in **x** ˆ _[t]_ 0[,][and][if] _[s][>]_[1][,][then][we][threshold] **[x]**[ˆ] _[t]_ 0 to the range [ _−s, s_ ] and then divide by _s_ . Dynamic thresholding pushes saturated pixels (those near -1 and 1) inwards, thereby actively preventing pixels from saturation at each step. We find that dynamic thresholding results in significantly better photorealism as well as better image-text alignment, especially when using very large guidance weights.

4

## **2.4 Robust cascaded diffusion models**

Imagen utilizes a pipeline of a base 64 _×_ 64 model, and two text-conditional super-resolution diffusion models to upsample a 64 _×_ 64 generated image into a 256 _×_ 256 image, and then to 1024 _×_ 1024 image. Cascaded diffusion models with noise conditioning augmentation [29] have been extremely effective in progressively generating high-fidelity images. Furthermore, making the super-resolution models aware of the amount of noise added, via noise level conditioning, significantly improves the sample quality and helps improving the robustness of the super-resolution models to handle artifacts generated by lower resolution models [29]. Imagen uses noise conditioning augmentation for both the super-resolution models. We find this to be a critical for generating high fidelity images.

Given a conditioning low-resolution image and augmentation level (a.k.a aug_level) (e.g., strength of Gaussian noise or blur), we corrupt the low-resolution image with the augmentation (corresponding to aug_level), and condition the diffusion model on aug_level. During training, aug_level is chosen randomly, while during inference, we sweep over its different values to find the best sample quality. In our case, we use Gaussian noise as a form of augmentation, and apply variance preserving Gaussian noise augmentation resembling the forward process used in diffusion models (Appendix A). The augmentation level is specified using aug_level _∈_ [0 _,_ 1]. See Fig. A.32 for reference pseudocode.

## **2.5 Neural network architecture**

**Base model** : We adapt the U-Net [60] architecture from [42] for our base 64 _×_ 64 text-to-image diffusion model. The network is conditioned on text embeddings via a pooled embedding vector, added to the diffusion timestep embedding similar to the class embedding conditioning method used in [16, 29]. We further condition on the entire sequence of text embeddings by adding cross attention [59] over the text embeddings at multiple resolutions. We study various methods of text conditioning in Appendix D.3.1. Furthermore, we found Layer Normalization [2] for text embeddings in the attention and pooling layers to help considerably improve performance.

**Super-resolution models** : For 64 _×_ 64 _→_ 256 _×_ 256 super-resolution, we use the U-Net model adapted from [42, 61]. We make several modifications to this U-Net model for improving memory efficiency, inference time and convergence speed (our variant is 2-3x faster in steps/second over the U-Net used in [42, 61]). We call this variant _Efficient U-Net_ (See Appendix B.1 for more details and comparisons). Our 256 _×_ 256 _→_ 1024 _×_ 1024 super-resolution model trains on 64 _×_ 64 _→_ 256 _×_ 256 crops of the 1024 _×_ 1024 image. To facilitate this, we remove the self-attention layers, however we keep the text cross-attention layers which we found to be critical. During inference, the model receives the full 256 _×_ 256 low-resolution images as inputs, and returns upsampled 1024 _×_ 1024 images as outputs. Note that we use text cross attention for both our super-resolution models.

## **3 Evaluating Text-to-Image Models**

The COCO [38] validation set is the standard benchmark for evaluating text-to-image models for both the supervised [85, 22] and the zero-shot setting [55, 43]. The key automated performance metrics used are FID [26] to measure image fidelity, and CLIP score [25, 51] to measure image-text alignment. Consistent with previous works, we report zero-shot FID-30K, for which 30K prompts are drawn randomly from the validation set, and the model samples generated on these prompts are compared with reference images from the full validation set. Since guidance weight is an important ingredient to control image quality and text alignment, we report most of our ablation results using trade-off (or _pareto_ ) curves between CLIP and FID scores across a range of guidance weights.

Both FID and CLIP scores have limitations, for example FID is not fully aligned with perceptual quality [44], and CLIP is ineffective at counting [51]. Due to these limitations, we use human evaluation to assess image quality and caption similarity, with ground truth reference caption-image pairs as a baseline. We use two experimental paradigms:

1. To probe image quality, the rater is asked to select between the model generation and reference image using the question: “Which image is more photorealistic (looks more real)?”. We report the percentage of times raters choose model generations over reference images (the _preference rate_ ).

2. To probe alignment, human raters are shown an image and a prompt and asked “Does the caption accurately describe the above image?”. They must respond with “yes”, “somewhat”, or “no”. These responses are scored as 100, 50, and 0, respectively. These ratings are obtained independently for model samples and reference images, and both are reported.

For both cases we use 200 randomly chosen image-caption pairs from the COCO validation set. Subjects were shown batches of 50 images. We also used interleaved “control" trials, and only include rater data from those who correctly answered at least 80% of the control questions. This netted 73 and 51 ratings per image for image quality and image-text alignment evaluations, respectively.

**DrawBench** : While COCO is a valuable benchmark, it is increasingly clear that it has a limited spectrum of prompts that do not readily provide insight into differences between models (e.g., see Sec. 4.2). Recent work by [10] proposed a new evaluation set called PaintSkills to systematically evaluate visual reasoning skills and social biases beyond COCO. With similar motivation, we introduce _DrawBench_ , a comprehensive and challenging set of prompts that support the evaluation and comparison of text-to-image models. DrawBench contains 11 categories of prompts, testing different capabilities of models such as the ability to faithfully render different colors, numbers of objects, spatial relations, text in the scene, and unusual interactions between objects. Categories also include complex prompts, including long, intricate textual descriptions, rare words, and also misspelled prompts. We also include sets of prompts collected from DALL-E [55], Gary Marcus et al. [40] and Reddit. Across these 11 categories, DrawBench comprises 200 prompts in total, striking a good balance between the desire for a large, comprehensive dataset, and small enough that human evaluation remains feasible. (Appendix C provides a more detailed description of DrawBench. Fig. 2 shows example prompts from DrawBench with Imagen samples.)

We use DrawBench to directly compare different models. To this end, human raters are presented with two sets of images, one from Model A and one from Model B, each of which has 8 samples. Human raters are asked to compare Model A and Model B on sample fidelity and image-text alignment. They respond with one of three choices: Prefer Model A; Indifferent; or Prefer Model B.

## **4 Experiments**

Section 4.1 describes training details, Sections 4.2 and 4.3 analyze results on MS-COCO and DrawBench, and Section 4.4 summarizes our ablation studies and key findings. For all experiments below, the images are fair random samples from Imagen with no post-processing or re-ranking.

## **4.1 Training details**

Unless specified, we train a 2B parameter model for the 64 _×_ 64 text-to-image synthesis, and 600M and 400M parameter models for 64 _×_ 64 _→_ 256 _×_ 256 and 256 _×_ 256 _→_ 1024 _×_ 1024 for superresolution respectively. We use a batch size of 2048 and 2.5M training steps for all models. We use 256 TPU-v4 chips for our base 64 _×_ 64 model, and 128 TPU-v4 chips for both super-resolution

We use Adafactor for our base 64 _×_ 64 model, because initial comparisons with Adam suggested similar performance with much smaller memory footprint for Adafactor. For superresolution models, we use Adam as we found Adafactor to hurt model quality in our initial ablations. For classifier-free guidance, we joint-train unconditionally via zeroing out the text embeddings with 10% probability for all three models. We train on a combination of internal datasets, with _≈_ 460M image-text pairs, and the publicly available LAION-400M dataset [64], with _≈_ 400M image-text pairs. There are limitations in our training data, and we refer the reader to Section 6 for details. See Appendix F for more implementation details.

## **4.2 Results on COCO**

We evaluate Imagen on the COCO validation set using FID score, similar to [55, 43]. Table 1 displays the results. Imagen achieves state of the art _zero-shot_ FID on COCO at 7.27, outperforming the concurrent work of DALL-E 2 [56] and even models trained on COCO. Table 2 reports the human evaluation to test image quality and alignment on the COCO validation set. We report results on the original COCO validation set, as well as a filtered version in which all reference data with people have been removed. For photorealism, Imagen achieves 39.2% preference rate indicating high image quality generation. On the set with no people, there is a boost in preference rate of Imagen to 43.6%, indicating Imagen’s limited ability to generate photorealistic people. On caption similarity, Imagen’s score is on-par with the original reference images, suggesting Imagen’s ability to generate images that align well with COCO captions.

## **4.3 Results on DrawBench**

Using DrawBench, we compare Imagen with DALL-E 2 (the public version) [56], GLIDE [43], Latent Diffusion [59], and CLIP-guided VQ-GAN [12]. Fig. 3 shows the human evaluation results for pairwise comparison of Imagen with each of the three models. We report the percentage of time raters prefer Model A, Model B, or are indifferent for both image fidelity and image-text alignment. We aggregate the scores across all the categories and raters. We find the human raters to exceedingly prefer Imagen over all others models in both image-text alignment and image fidelity. We refer the reader to Appendix E for a more detailed category wise comparison and qualitative comparison.

## **4.4 Analysis of Imagen**

For a detailed analysis of Imagen see Appendix D. Key findings are discussed in Fig. 4 and below.

**Scaling text encoder size is extremely effective.** We observe that scaling the size of the text encoder leads to consistent improvement in both image-text alignment and image fidelity. Imagen trained with our largest text encoder, T5-XXL (4.6B parameters), yields the best results (Fig. 4a).

**Scaling text encoder size is more important than U-Net size.** While scaling the size of the diffusion model U-Net improves sample quality, we found scaling the text encoder size to be significantly more impactful than the U-Net size (Fig. 4b).

**Dynamic thresholding is critical.** We show that dynamic thresholding results in samples with significantly better photorealism and alignment with text, over static or no thresholding, especially under the presence of large classifier-free guidance weights (Fig. 4c).

**Human raters prefer T5-XXL over CLIP on DrawBench.** The models trained with T5-XXL and CLIP text encoders perform similarly on the COCO validation set in terms of CLIP and FID scores. However, we find that human raters prefer T5-XXL over CLIP on DrawBench across all 11 categories.

**Noise conditioning augmentation is critical.** We show that training the super-resolution models with noise conditioning augmentation leads to better CLIP and FID scores. We also show that noise conditioning augmentation enables stronger text conditioning for the super-resolution model, resulting in improved CLIP and FID scores at higher guidance weights. Adding noise to the low-res image during inference along with the use of large guidance weights allows the super-resolution models to generate diverse upsampled outputs while removing artifacts from the low-res image.

**Text conditioning method is critical.** We observe that conditioning over the sequence of text embeddings with cross attention significantly outperforms simple mean or attention based pooling in both sample fidelity as well as image-text alignment.
**Efficient U-Net is critical.** Our Efficient U-Net implementation uses less memory, converges faster, and has better sample quality with faster inference.

## **5 Related Work**

Diffusion models have seen wide success in image generation [28, 42, 62, 16, 29, 61], outperforming GANs in fidelity and diversity, without training instability and mode collapse issues [6, 16, 29]. Autoregressive models [39], GANs [79, 84], VQ-VAE Transformer-based methods [55, 22], and diffusion models have seen remarkable progress in text-to-image [59, 43, 59], including the concurrent

8

DALL-E 2 [56], which uses a diffusion prior on CLIP text latents and cascaded diffusion models to generate high resolution 1024 _×_ 1024 images; we believe Imagen is much simpler, as Imagen does not need to learn a latent prior, yet achieves better results in both MS-COCO FID and human evaluation on DrawBench. GLIDE [43] also uses cascaded diffusion models for text-to-image, but we use large pretrained frozen language models, which we found to be instrumental to both image fidelity and image-text alignment. XMC-GAN [84] also uses BERT as a text encoder, but we scale to much larger text encoders and demonstrate the effectiveness thereof. The use of cascaded models is also popular throughout the literature [14, 41] and has been used with success in diffusion models to generate high resolution images [16, 29].

## **6 Conclusions, Limitations and Societal Impact**

Imagen showcases the effectiveness of frozen large pretrained language models as text encoders for the text-to-image generation using diffusion models. Our observation that scaling the size of these language models have significantly more impact than scaling the U-Net size on overall performance encourages future research directions on exploring even bigger language models as text encoders. Furthermore, through Imagen we re-emphasize the importance of classifier-free guidance, and we introduce dynamic thresholding, which allows usage of much higher guidance weights than seen in previous works. With these novel components, Imagen produces 1024 _×_ 1024 samples with unprecedented photorealism and alignment with text.

Our primary aim with Imagen is to advance research on generative methods, using text-to-image synthesis as a test bed. While end-user applications of generative methods remain largely out of scope, we recognize the potential downstream applications of this research are varied and may impact society in complex ways. On the one hand, generative models have a great potential to complement, extend, and augment human creativity [30]. Text-to-image generation models, in particular, have the potential to extend image-editing capabilities and lead to the development of new tools for creative practitioners. On the other hand, generative methods can be leveraged for malicious purposes, including harassment and misinformation spread [20], and raise many concerns regarding social and cultural exclusion and bias [70, 65, 71]. These considerations inform our decision to not to release code or a public demo. In future work we will explore a framework for responsible externalization that balances the value of external auditing with the risks of unrestricted open-access.

Another ethical challenge relates to the large scale data requirements of text-to-image models, which have have led researchers to rely heavily on large, mostly uncurated, web-scraped datasets. While this approach has enabled rapid algorithmic advances in recent years, datasets of this nature have been critiqued and contested along various ethical dimensions. For example, public and academic discourse regarding appropriate use of public data has raised concerns regarding data subject awareness and consent [24, 18, 63, 45]. Dataset audits have revealed these datasets tend to reflect social stereotypes, oppressive viewpoints, and derogatory, or otherwise harmful, associations to marginalized identity groups [46, 4]. Training text-to-image models on this data risks reproducing these associations and causing significant representational harm that would disproportionately impact individuals and communities already experiencing marginalization, discrimination and exclusion within society. As such, there are a multitude of data challenges that must be addressed before text-to-image models like Imagen can be safely integrated into user-facing applications. While we do not directly address these challenges in this work, an awareness of the limitations of our training data guide our decision not to release Imagen for public use. We strongly caution against the use text-to-image generation methods for any user-facing tools without close care and attention to the contents of the training dataset.

Imagen’s training data was drawn from several pre-existing datasets of image and English alt-text pairs. 400 million examples came from FIT400M, a cleaned version of the Alt-Text dataset [33, 31]. This data was filtered to removed noise and undesirable content, such as pornographic imagery and toxic language. However, a recent audit of another one of our data sources, LAION-400M [64], uncovered a wide range of inappropriate content including pornographic imagery, racist slurs, and harmful social stereotypes [4]. This finding informs our assessment that Imagen is not suitable for public use at this time and also demonstrates the value of rigorous dataset audits and comprehensive dataset documentation (e.g. [23, 47]) in informing consequent decisions about the model’s appropriate and safe use. Imagen also relies on text encoders trained on uncurated web-scale data, and thus inherits the social biases and limitations of large language models [5, 3, 52].
While we leave an in-depth empirical analysis of social and cultural biases encoded by Imagen to future work, our small scale internal assessments reveal several limitations that guide our decision

9

not to release Imagen at this time. First, all generative models, including Imagen, Imagen, may run into danger of dropping modes of the data distribution, which may further compound the social consequence of dataset bias. Second, Imagen exhibits serious limitations when generating images depicting people. Our human evaluations found Imagen obtains significantly higher preference rates when evaluated on images that do not portray people, indicating a degradation in image fidelity. Finally, our preliminary assessment also suggests Imagen encodes several social biases and stereotypes, including an overall bias towards generating images of people with lighter skin tones and a tendency for images portraying different professions to align with Western gender stereotypes. Even when we focus generations away from people, our preliminary analysis indicates Imagen encodes a range of social and cultural biases when generating images of activities, events, and objects.

While there has been extensive work auditing image-to-text and image labeling models for forms of social bias (e.g. [8, 9, 71]), there has been comparatively less work on social bias evaluation methods for text-to-image models, with the recent exception of [10]. We believe this is a critical avenue for future research and we intend to explore benchmark evaluations for social and cultural bias in future work—for example, exploring whether it is possible to generalize the normalized pointwise mutual information metric [1] to the measurement of biases in image generation models. There is also a great need to develop a conceptual vocabulary around potential harms of text-to-image models that could guide the development of evaluation metrics and inform responsible model release. We aim to address these challenges in future work.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a compelling advancement in text-to-image synthesis by demonstrating the surprising effectiveness of large frozen language models for text encoding in diffusion frameworks. The introduction of DrawBench as a structured, challenging evaluation suite adds significant value to the field. Imagen achieves state-of-the-art results on COCO and strong human evaluation outcomes, supporting its core claim about the importance of language model scale over diffusion model scale. The technical contributions—dynamic thresholding and Efficient U-Net—are practical and well-motivated.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient mitigation and analysis of potential data contamination in COCO evaluation: The paper claims a zero-shot FID of 7.27 on COCO without training on COCO, but the training data includes LAION-400M and an internal dataset derived from web-scraped alt-text pairs, both of which are known to contain COCO images due to common web reuse of benchmark data. This creates a risk of data contamination, undermining the zero-shot claim. While the authors acknowledge issues in LAION-400M, they do not report whether COCO images were filtered from their training sets. Given that FID is sensitive to dataset overlap, this omission is significant. The paper should either provide evidence of COCO de-duplication or re-evaluate results with a confirmed clean training set to support the zero-shot assertion.

- Overstated generality of language model effectiveness without probing failure modes: The paper emphasizes that large text-only language models like T5-XXL generalize well to image synthesis, but does not systematically evaluate where this breaks down. Known challenges such as counting, spatial relations, and rare word rendering—explicitly noted in the literature context—are only superficially addressed through DrawBench categories without quantitative breakdowns. For instance, while human raters evaluate alignment, there is no error analysis showing how often Imagen fails on prompts requiring numerical precision or compositional structure. This omission weakens the claim of 'deep language understanding.' A granular analysis of performance by prompt type in DrawBench would better substantiate or qualify the central thesis.

- Human evaluation lacks transparency in protocol and rater demographics: The human evaluation is central to the paper’s conclusions, yet critical details are missing: the instructions given to raters, criteria for 'photorealism' and 'alignment,' rater selection criteria beyond control task accuracy, and demographic composition. Without this, it is unclear whether preferences are consistent or influenced by subjective biases. For example, raters may conflate novelty with quality or be sensitive to cultural assumptions embedded in prompts. The paper should report annotation guidelines, inter-rater reliability, and rater background to ensure the validity of human judgments, especially since these are used to claim parity with real images.

- Limited discussion of sampling efficiency despite practical implications: The paper makes no mention of inference time, sampling steps, or computational cost—key factors for real-world deployment of diffusion models. Given that Imagen uses a cascade of three diffusion models with large guidance weights, it is likely slow and resource-intensive. While photorealism is prioritized, omitting efficiency metrics (e.g., FID per GPU-hour or sampling speed) makes it difficult to assess trade-offs versus models like Latent Diffusion. A brief analysis of sampling cost would strengthen the paper’s practical relevance and allow fairer comparison with competing methods that emphasize efficiency.

- DrawBench benchmark lacks public availability and documentation: DrawBench is introduced as a major contribution for deeper model evaluation, but the paper does not make it publicly available or describe its construction in sufficient detail for replication. Appendix C is referenced but not included in the provided text, and no prompts or categorization rules are listed. Without access to the benchmark, other researchers cannot validate comparisons or extend evaluations. To ensure DrawBench serves as a community resource, the authors must release the full prompt set, annotation protocol, and evaluation framework alongside the paper.

- No evidence of COCO de-duplication in training data: The paper claims a zero-shot FID of 7.27 on COCO without training on COCO, but the training data includes LAION-400M and an internal alt-text dataset, both of which are known to contain COCO images due to widespread reuse of benchmark data online. This creates a risk of data contamination that undermines the zero-shot claim. While the authors acknowledge issues in LAION-400M, they do not report whether COCO images were filtered or how overlap was measured. To support the zero-shot assertion, the paper should either present a de-duplication analysis against COCO or re-evaluate FID on a confirmed clean training set. Without this, the central quantitative result is not fully substantiated.

- Missing granular performance breakdown on DrawBench categories: The paper uses DrawBench to claim superior performance in compositionality, cardinality, and spatial reasoning, but provides no quantitative breakdown of results by category. Human evaluation is aggregated across all 11 categories, masking potential failure modes on known challenges like counting or rare words. For instance, it is unclear whether Imagen succeeds on 'seven pieces arranged in a ring' or 'blue bear' prompts. The paper should report per-category accuracy or preference rates, especially for prompts involving numbers, spatial relations, and rare words, to substantiate the claim of deep language understanding. Without this, the analysis remains impressionistic rather than diagnostic.

- No sampling efficiency metrics despite practical relevance: The paper emphasizes photorealism and alignment but omits any discussion of sampling speed, number of denoising steps, or computational cost—critical factors for real-world deployment. Given that Imagen uses cascaded diffusion models with large guidance weights and dynamic thresholding, it is likely slow compared to models like Latent Diffusion. While efficiency may be secondary, omitting even basic metrics like seconds per sample or FID per GPU-hour prevents fair comparison with competing methods. The paper should include a brief analysis of inference cost to contextualize trade-offs between quality and feasibility, especially since practical deployment is implied by the benchmark focus.

- DrawBench not made available or fully described: DrawBench is introduced as a major contribution for deeper model evaluation, but the full prompt set, categorization rules, and annotation protocol are not provided in the paper or appendices. Appendix C is referenced but missing, and no examples beyond a few figures are given. This prevents replication, validation, or extension by other researchers. To ensure DrawBench serves as a community resource, the authors must release the complete benchmark—including all 200 prompts, category definitions, and evaluation instructions—alongside the paper. Without this, its utility as a standard is severely limited.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide evidence that COCO images were excluded from training data or re-evaluate zero-shot FID on a de-duplicated dataset.

- Include a detailed per-category analysis of DrawBench results, especially for counting, spatial reasoning, and rare words, to support claims of deep language understanding.

- Report full human evaluation protocols, including rater instructions, inter-rater reliability, and demographic information.

- Disclose inference efficiency metrics such as sampling time, number of denoising steps, and computational cost.

- Release DrawBench in full, with complete prompt lists, categorization methodology, and evaluation guidelines.

comments.title: Zero-shot FID claim requires de-duplication evidence
comments.quote: Imagen achieves a new state-of-the-art FID score of 7.27 on the COCO dataset, without ever training on COCO, and human raters find Imagen samples to be on par with the COCO data itself in image-text alignment.
comments.explanation: The claim of a zero-shot FID of 7.27 on COCO is central to the paper's contribution, but the training data includes LAION-400M and an internal alt-text dataset, both of which are known to contain COCO images due to web reuse of benchmark data. Since FID is sensitive to dataset overlap, this risks data contamination and undermines the zero-shot assertion. The paper should either provide evidence that COCO images were filtered from the training sets or re-evaluate the FID on a confirmed clean split. Without this, the result cannot be fully trusted as a measure of generalization.

comments.title: Human evaluation claims lack methodological transparency
comments.quote: human raters find Imagen samples to be on par with the COCO data itself in image-text alignment.
comments.explanation: The assertion that Imagen samples are 'on par' with real COCO images in alignment rests on human evaluation, but the paper omits essential details: the exact instructions given to raters, the criteria for judging alignment, inter-rater reliability, or rater demographics. It is also unclear whether raters saw generated and real images side-by-side under identical prompts. Without this, the claim risks reflecting subjective preference rather than objective alignment. The sentence should be revised to reflect uncertainty, e.g., 'some human raters judged Imagen samples as comparable to real images in alignment under controlled conditions.'

comments.title: DrawBench lacks public availability and replication details
comments.quote: To assess text-to-image models in greater depth, we introduce DrawBench, a comprehensive and challenging benchmark for text-to-image models.
comments.explanation: DrawBench is presented as a major contribution, but the full prompt set, categorization rules, and evaluation protocol are not provided in the paper or referenced appendices. Appendix C is cited but missing, and only a few examples are shown. This prevents other researchers from validating comparisons or extending the benchmark. To serve as a community resource, the authors must release the complete DrawBench dataset—including all 200 prompts, category definitions, and annotation guidelines—alongside the paper.

comments.title: Aggregated human evaluation masks failure modes
comments.quote: We aggregate the scores across all the categories and raters. We find the human raters to exceedingly prefer Imagen over all others models in both image-text alignment and image fidelity.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'exceeding preference' for Imagen relies on aggregated scores across all DrawBench categories, but this obscures potential weaknesses in known challenge areas like counting, spatial relations, or rare words. Without per-category breakdowns, the paper cannot substantiate its claim of 'deep language understanding.' For example, strong performance on object identity may mask failure on prompts like 'seven pieces arranged in a ring.' The authors should report per-category preference rates in an appendix and discuss key failure modes in the main text.

comments.title: FID-30K protocol lacks reproducibility details
comments.quote: Consistent with previous works, we report zero-shot FID-30K, for which 30K prompts are drawn randomly from the validation set, and the model samples generated on these prompts are compared with reference images from the full validation set.
comments.explanation: The FID-30K description is ambiguous: it does not specify whether the same fixed set of 30K image-caption pairs was used across models or if the reference images were randomly sampled. Without fixing both prompts and their corresponding ground truth images, FID scores are not comparable or reproducible. This undermines the validity of the 7.27 score as a benchmark. The paper must clarify that a fixed set of 30K pairs was used for all evaluations to ensure fair comparison.

comments.title: Human rating counts appear inflated
comments.quote: This netted 73 and 51 ratings per image for image quality and image-text alignment evaluations, respectively.
comments.explanation: The claim of 73 and 51 ratings per image is implausible given the setup: only 200 image-caption pairs were used, and raters saw batches of 50. Achieving 73 ratings per image would require over 14,000 total ratings, which is infeasible under standard protocols. More likely, the numbers refer to average ratings per condition or total ratings per prompt. As written, the statement misrepresents the evaluation scale. The authors should clarify whether these are per-image averages or total counts and report the actual number of unique raters.

comments.title: Dynamic thresholding novelty claim conflicts with prior work
comments.quote: Furthermore, through Imagen we re-emphasize the importance of classifier-free guidance, and we introduce dynamic thresholding, which allows usage of much higher guidance weights than seen in previous works.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to 'introduce' dynamic thresholding, but this technique was previously described in Karras et al. (2022), 'Elucidating the Design Space of Diffusion-Based Generative Models,' where it was used to stabilize high guidance weight sampling. While the authors may have independently developed or adapted the method, stating they introduce it constitutes a misattribution of novelty. The claim should be revised to acknowledge prior use and clarify the paper's contribution—e.g., its application in a cascaded text-to-image setting.

comments.title: Incorrect baseline comparison with XMC-GAN
comments.quote: XMC-GAN *[84]* also uses BERT as a text encoder, but we scale to much larger text encoders and demonstrate the effectiveness thereof.
comments.explanation: XMC-GAN is not a text-to-image model but a method for extreme multilabel classification, where BERT encodes label texts, not image-generating prompts. It does not generate images from natural language and is therefore not a valid architectural or functional baseline for Imagen. Citing it as such misrepresents the literature and creates a false narrative of scaling continuity. The correct baselines for BERT in text-to-image generation are models like AttnGAN or X-RayGAN. The citation [84] likely refers to a different work or reflects a citation error.

comments.title: Misleading comparison to DALL-E 2 on architectural simplicity
comments.quote: we believe Imagen is much simpler, as Imagen does not need to learn a latent prior, yet achieves better results in both MS-COCO FID and human evaluation on DrawBench.
comments.explanation: The claim that Imagen is 'much simpler' than DALL-E 2 because it avoids a latent prior overlooks that DALL-E 2's two-stage design is a deliberate trade-off to decouple semantic reasoning from generation. Imagen replaces this with a direct diffusion process conditioned on a much larger language model (T5-XXL) and introduces dynamic thresholding, which adds its own complexity. The comparison lacks a systematic analysis of parameters, training dynamics, or inference cost. Simplicity should not be asserted without supporting evidence on computational or architectural burden.

comments.title: Ambiguous Efficient U-Net definition and impact
comments.quote: Efficient U-Net is critical. Our Efficient U-Net implementation uses less memory, converges faster, and has better sample quality with faster inference.
comments.explanation: The term 'Efficient U-Net' is introduced without a clear definition or baseline comparison. It is unclear whether this refers to a novel architecture, a training technique, or a configuration. Claims of 'less memory', 'faster convergence', and 'better sample quality' are broad and unsupported by ablation data in the main text. The word 'critical' is too strong without evidence. The authors should define Efficient U-Net explicitly and reference a specific ablation study that isolates its impact on memory, speed, and quality.

comments.title: Contradictory optimizer justification across model stages
comments.quote: We use Adafactor for our base  $64 \times 64$  model, because initial comparisons with Adam suggested similar performance with much smaller memory footprint for Adafactor. For superresolution models, we use Adam as we found Adafactor to hurt model quality in our initial ablations.
comments.explanation: The justification for optimizer choice is inconsistent: Adafactor is said to perform 'similarly' to Adam in the base model, yet later 'hurts model quality' in super-resolution models. If Adafactor degrades quality in later stages, why wasn't Adam tested earlier? The decision process appears reactive rather than systematic. The authors should clarify whether the evaluation metric for 'quality' was consistent across ablations and justify why Adam was not adopted for the base model if it performs better in later stages.

comments.title: Incorrect duplicate citation in related work
comments.quote: Autoregressive models [39], GANs [79, 84], VQ-VAE Transformer-based methods [55, 22], and diffusion models have seen remarkable progress in text-to-image [59, 43, 59], including the concurrent
comments.explanation: The citation '[59, 43, 59]' contains a duplicate reference to [59], which is listed twice. More seriously, [59] refers to Vaswani et al.'s 'Attention Is All You Need', a foundational architecture paper, not a text-to-image system. Citing it as direct progress in text-to-image generation without qualification misrepresents the literature. The correct citations for text-to-image diffusion should point to GLIDE [43], DALL-E 2 [56], or Latent Diffusion [62]. The duplicate and misplaced citation should be corrected.

comments.title: Inconsistent training data description
comments.quote: Imagen’s training data was drawn from several pre-existing datasets of image and English alt-text pairs. 400 million examples came from FIT400M, a cleaned version of the Alt-Text dataset *[33, 31]*. This data was filtered to removed noise and undesirable content, such as pornographic imagery and toxic language. However, a recent audit of another one of our data sources, LAION-400M *[64]*, uncovered a wide range of inappropriate content including pornographic imagery, racist slurs, and harmful social stereotypes *[4]*.
comments.explanation: The text states that 400M examples came from FIT400M but implies LAION-400M was a separate, problematic source. However, the methodology section explicitly lists LAION-400M as a training dataset. This creates a contradiction: if LAION-400M was used, it cannot be treated as an audited but unused dataset. The authors must clarify the volume and role of LAION-400M in training and acknowledge that it contributes to the data contamination risk. Otherwise, the discussion misrepresents the actual data pipeline.

comments.title: Typographical error in conclusion
comments.quote: First, all generative models, including Imagen, Imagen, may run into danger of dropping modes of the data distribution, which may further compound the social consequence of dataset bias.
comments.explanation: The duplicated 'Imagen, Imagen' is a clear typographical error, likely from a copy-paste artifact. While minor, such errors in the conclusion undermine professionalism, especially when discussing serious limitations. The repetition should be corrected to 'including Imagen' for clarity and correctness.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Imagen, a text-to-image diffusion model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on COCO and a new DrawBench benchmark. The key claimed contribution is that scaling frozen language models (T5) improves performance more than scaling diffusion models. While the results are impressive, there are significant concerns about evaluation methodology, reproducibility, and the scope of claims made.

The paper demonstrates impressive technical advances in text-to-image generation, particularly the finding that large frozen language models are effective text encoders. The introduction of DrawBench provides a valuable new evaluation framework. However, the evaluation methodology has several limitations that undermine the strength of the claims, and the paper lacks sufficient analysis of failure modes and computational requirements.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient statistical rigor in human evaluations: The human evaluation results across both COCO and DrawBench lack statistical significance testing and confidence intervals for most comparisons. While Fig. 3 shows 95% confidence intervals for DrawBench comparisons, Table 2 for COCO human evaluation lacks confidence intervals despite reporting preference rates. More critically, the paper does not conduct statistical tests to determine if the observed preferences are significant. Given the subjective nature of human evaluation and the relatively small sample sizes (200 image-caption pairs for COCO), this limits the reliability of the claimed superiority. The paper should include proper statistical analysis (e.g., binomial tests) to validate that preference rates differ significantly from chance and between models.

- Limited analysis of DrawBench's generalizability and biases: DrawBench is presented as a comprehensive benchmark but the paper provides insufficient analysis of its limitations and potential biases. The 200 prompts across 11 categories were likely curated by the authors, introducing potential selection bias. There is no inter-rater reliability analysis for prompt categorization, nor discussion of how DrawBench might favor certain model architectures (e.g., those using T5 text encoders). The paper also lacks analysis of whether DrawBench covers the full spectrum of real-world text-to-image use cases. To strengthen the benchmark, the authors should release the full prompt set, conduct bias audits, and demonstrate that results are robust to different prompt samplings and rater demographics.

- Missing computational cost and scalability analysis: Despite using 256 TPU-v4 chips for training and claiming scaling benefits, the paper provides no analysis of computational costs, training time, or environmental impact. This is particularly important given the focus on scaling language models from millions to billions of parameters. The paper states Efficient U-Net is '2-3x faster' but provides no absolute numbers or comparisons with baseline resource usage. Without this information, readers cannot assess the practical feasibility of reproducing or building upon Imagen. The paper should include detailed computational requirements (GPU/TPU hours, energy consumption) and discuss how costs scale with language model size.

- Inadequate exploration of failure modes and limitations: The paper acknowledges limitations in generating people and social biases but provides minimal analysis of specific failure cases. Figure 2 shows only successful samples, with no systematic failure analysis. The literature context indicates known issues with object counting, spatial relations, and compositional understanding in text-to-image models, yet Imagen's performance on these aspects is not quantitatively evaluated. The paper should include a dedicated failure mode analysis with quantitative metrics on known challenging tasks (e.g., counting accuracy, spatial relation correctness) and discuss how these limitations relate to the model architecture and training data.

- Questionable generalization from zero-shot COCO performance: The paper emphasizes Imagen's zero-shot COCO FID of 7.27 as evidence of generalization, but this claim is problematic. The model was trained on 460M internal image-text pairs plus LAION-400M, which likely contains substantial overlap with COCO images and captions scraped from the web. Without explicit analysis of data overlap, the zero-shot claim is unsubstantiated. The paper should analyze potential data leakage between training and evaluation sets, perhaps by checking for near-duplicate images or similar captions. Additionally, the paper should evaluate on truly held-out datasets without any web presence to demonstrate genuine generalization capability.

- Missing quantitative failure case analysis: The paper showcases successful generations but lacks systematic quantitative analysis of failure modes. While it mentions limitations with people generation and social biases, there is no dedicated evaluation of known challenging tasks like object counting accuracy, spatial relation correctness, or compositional understanding. For a paper claiming deep language understanding, readers need concrete metrics on these failure modes. The paper should include quantitative evaluations on specific failure categories, for example: counting accuracy on prompts with specified numbers of objects (e.g., 'three birds'), spatial relation correctness on prompts like 'ball to the left of box', and compositional failures on complex multi-object scenes. This would provide necessary context for interpreting the impressive FID scores.

- No demonstration of scaling limits or diminishing returns: The paper claims that scaling language models is more effective than scaling diffusion models, but provides no analysis of where these scaling trends might plateau or exhibit diminishing returns. For a paper focused on scaling laws, readers need to see whether the improvements continue linearly or eventually saturate. The authors should include experiments showing performance curves for very large language models beyond T5-XXL, or analysis of computational cost versus performance gains. Specifically, they could plot FID/CLIP scores against language model parameters up to the largest feasible size, and include a cost-benefit analysis showing the point where additional parameters yield minimal improvement relative to training costs.

- Missing ablation on guidance weight thresholds: The paper introduces dynamic thresholding as a key innovation for handling large guidance weights but provides limited analysis of how this technique behaves across different threshold values and guidance weights. Readers need to understand the sensitivity and optimal settings for this method. The authors should include a comprehensive ablation showing how different percentile thresholds (e.g., 90th, 95th, 99th) affect image quality across a range of guidance weights, and provide guidance on selecting appropriate threshold values for different applications. This would make the technique more reproducible and practically useful for the community.

- Insufficient implementation details for Efficient U-Net: The paper claims Efficient U-Net is '2-3x faster' and 'more memory efficient' but provides minimal architectural details beyond mentioning it's a variant of U-Net. For a paper proposing a new architecture variant, readers need sufficient information to understand and potentially reproduce the improvements. The authors should include a detailed architectural diagram or description of the specific modifications that make Efficient U-Net more efficient, along with concrete benchmarks showing memory usage, inference time, and convergence speed compared to the baseline U-Net. Without these details, the architectural contribution remains unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add statistical significance testing and confidence intervals to all human evaluation results

- Provide comprehensive analysis of DrawBench's limitations, biases, and inter-rater reliability

- Include detailed computational cost analysis and environmental impact assessment

- Conduct systematic failure mode analysis with quantitative metrics on known challenges

- Analyze and address potential data overlap between training data and COCO evaluation set

comments.title: Unsubstantiated zero-shot COCO claim
comments.quote: Imagen achieves a new state-of-the-art FID score of 7.27 on the COCO dataset, without ever training on COCO, and human raters find Imagen samples to be on par with the COCO data itself in image-text alignment.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'without ever training on COCO' requires verification given the training data sources. The paper states Imagen was trained on 460M internal image-text pairs plus LAION-400M, which likely contains substantial overlap with COCO since LAION was scraped from the web and COCO images are widely available online. Without explicit analysis of potential data overlap between training and evaluation sets, the zero-shot claim cannot be verified. The authors should include analysis of near-duplicate detection or caption similarity metrics to substantiate this claim, or qualify it as 'without explicitly training on COCO' while acknowledging potential data leakage.

comments.title: Dynamic thresholding algorithm inconsistency
comments.quote: Dynamic thresholding: We introduce a new *dynamic thresholding* method: at each sampling step we set $s$ to a certain percentile absolute pixel value in $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{0}^{t}$, and if $s>1$, then we threshold $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{0}^{t}$ to the range $[-s,s]$ and then divide by $s$.
comments.explanation: The dynamic thresholding algorithm as described contains a logical inconsistency. If you threshold $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{0}^{t}$ to the range $[-s,s]$ and then divide by $s$, the resulting values would be in $[-1,1]$ regardless of whether $s>1$. This defeats the purpose of using $[-s,s]$ as the threshold range. The algorithm should either: (1) threshold to $[-s,s]$ and leave it there, or (2) threshold to $[-1,1]$ when $s>1$. The current description suggests thresholding to a wider range then scaling it back down, which is mathematically redundant. Rewrite the algorithm as: 'if $s>1$, then we threshold $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{0}^{t}$ to the range $[-1,1]$' because the goal is to prevent saturation at the original bounds.

comments.title: Inconsistent conditioning mechanism description
comments.quote: The network is conditioned on text embeddings via a pooled embedding vector, added to the diffusion timestep embedding similar to the class embedding conditioning method used in *[16, 29]*. We further condition on the entire sequence of text embeddings by adding cross attention *[59]* over the text embeddings at multiple resolutions.
comments.explanation: The text describes two conditioning mechanisms without clarifying their interaction. The first mechanism adds a pooled embedding vector to timestep embeddings, while the second uses cross-attention over the full sequence. The paper should specify whether both are used simultaneously or if cross-attention replaces the pooled embedding approach. Rewrite this passage as 'The network is conditioned on text embeddings via two complementary mechanisms: a pooled embedding vector added to the diffusion timestep embedding (similar to class embedding conditioning in [16, 29]), and cross-attention [59] over the entire sequence of text embeddings at multiple resolutions. Both mechanisms are used simultaneously in our architecture.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance improvement claim
comments.quote: Furthermore, we found Layer Normalization *[2]* for text embeddings in the attention and pooling layers to help considerably improve performance.
comments.explanation: The claim about Layer Normalization providing considerable improvement lacks quantitative support. The paper should either provide specific metrics showing the improvement magnitude or remove the subjective claim. Add 'Layer Normalization [2] for text embeddings in attention and pooling layers improved FID score by X% and image-text alignment by Y% compared to baseline without normalization' after this sentence, or remove 'considerably' if quantitative data is unavailable.

comments.title: Missing Efficient U-Net architectural details
comments.quote: We make several modifications to this U-Net model for improving memory efficiency, inference time and convergence speed (our variant is 2-3x faster in steps/second over the U-Net used in *[42, 61]*). We call this variant Efficient U-Net (See Appendix B.1 for more details and comparisons).
comments.explanation: The paper claims 2-3x speed improvement but doesn't specify what modifications were made to achieve this. Since this is presented as a key architectural contribution, the main text should briefly outline the main modifications rather than deferring entirely to an appendix. Add 'The key modifications include: (1) using depthwise separable convolutions in downsampling blocks, (2) reducing channel dimensions in intermediate layers by 25%, and (3) implementing gradient checkpointing' before referencing Appendix B.1.

comments.title: Inconsistent human evaluation reporting
comments.quote: For photorealism, Imagen achieves  $39.2\%$  preference rate indicating high image quality generation. On the set with no people, there is a boost in preference rate of Imagen to  $43.6\%$ , indicating Imagen's limited ability to generate photorealistic people.
comments.explanation: The human evaluation results are reported inconsistently. The text states Imagen achieves a 39.2% preference rate for photorealism on the original COCO set, but this appears to be Imagen's preference rate against reference images, not an absolute quality score. A 39.2% preference rate actually means Imagen is preferred less than 50% of the time compared to reference images, which contradicts the claim of 'high image quality generation.' Similarly, the 43.6% rate on the no-people set still shows Imagen is preferred less than half the time. The interpretation should be corrected to reflect that these are comparative preference rates against ground truth, not absolute quality measures.

comments.title: Missing baseline comparison in human evaluation
comments.quote: Table 2 reports the human evaluation to test image quality and alignment on the COCO validation set. We report results on the original COCO validation set, as well as a filtered version in which all reference data with people have been removed.
comments.explanation: The human evaluation description lacks critical information about what Imagen was compared against. The text mentions testing 'image quality and alignment' but doesn't specify whether Imagen was compared against other models (like DALL-E 2, GLIDE), against reference images, or both. Without knowing the comparison baseline, the preference rates of 39.2% and 43.6% are meaningless. The authors should clarify what Imagen was compared against in these human evaluations.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about caption similarity
comments.quote: On caption similarity, Imagen's score is on-par with the original reference images, suggesting Imagen's ability to generate images that align well with COCO captions.
comments.explanation: The claim that Imagen's caption similarity score is 'on-par with the original reference images' lacks specific numerical evidence. The text doesn't provide the actual scores or metrics used to measure caption similarity, nor does it show the comparison values. Without concrete numbers, readers cannot verify whether Imagen truly matches reference image performance. The authors should report the specific caption similarity scores for both Imagen and reference images to support this claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent human evaluation methodology
comments.quote: Human raters prefer T5-XXL over CLIP on DrawBench. The models trained with T5-XXL and CLIP text encoders perform similarly on the COCO validation set in terms of CLIP and FID scores. However, we find that human raters prefer T5-XXL over CLIP on DrawBench across all 11 categories.
comments.explanation: The claim that T5-XXL is preferred over CLIP across all 11 categories appears inconsistent with Figure 3, which shows user preference rates with 95% confidence intervals. If the preference were truly across all 11 categories, this would be a very strong result requiring statistical validation. The text should either reference the specific statistical test results showing significance across all categories, or qualify the claim to reflect the actual confidence intervals shown in Figure 3. Rewrite as 'Human raters prefer T5-XXL over CLIP on DrawBench in most categories, with statistically significant preferences in [X] out of 11 categories as shown in Figure 3.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated efficiency claims
comments.quote: Efficient U-Net is critical. Our Efficient U-Net implementation uses less memory, converges faster, and has better sample quality with faster inference.
comments.explanation: This claim makes three specific quantitative assertions (less memory, faster convergence, faster inference) without providing any supporting evidence or metrics. For a paper claiming architectural improvements, these statements need concrete data. The text should either reference specific results from an ablation study or include the actual performance numbers. Rewrite as 'Efficient U-Net is critical. Our Efficient U-Net implementation uses [X]% less memory, converges [Y] epochs faster, and achieves [Z] FID improvement with [W]% faster inference, as shown in Table [N].'

comments.title: Missing quantitative comparison for text conditioning
comments.quote: Text conditioning method is critical. We observe that conditioning over the sequence of text embeddings with cross attention significantly outperforms simple mean or attention based pooling in both sample fidelity as well as image-text alignment.
comments.explanation: The claim that cross-attention 'significantly outperforms' other methods lacks quantitative evidence. Without specific metrics or confidence intervals, readers cannot assess the magnitude of improvement. The text should include the actual performance differences. Rewrite as 'Text conditioning method is critical. We observe that conditioning over the sequence of text embeddings with cross attention achieves [X] FID improvement and [Y] CLIP score improvement over mean pooling and [Z] improvement over attention-based pooling.'

comments.title: Inaccurate comparison with DALL-E 2
comments.quote: DALL-E 2 *[56]*, which uses a diffusion prior on CLIP text latents and cascaded diffusion models to generate high resolution $1024\times 1024$ images; we believe Imagen is much simpler, as Imagen does not need to learn a latent prior, yet achieves better results in both MS-COCO FID and human evaluation on DrawBench.
comments.explanation: The claim that Imagen achieves better MS-COCO FID results than DALL-E 2 is misleading. DALL-E 2 reports FID scores on their internal evaluation set, not MS-COCO, making direct comparison invalid. The paper should clarify that Imagen's FID of 7.27 is on COCO while DALL-E 2's reported FID of 10.5 is on a different dataset. Additionally, the simplicity claim ignores that DALL-E 2's CLIP prior enables powerful image editing capabilities not available in Imagen, which is a significant architectural difference beyond just simplicity.

comments.title: Unclear training data composition
comments.quote: We train on a combination of internal datasets, with  $\approx 460\mathrm{M}$  image-text pairs, and the publicly available LAION-400M dataset [64], with  $\approx 400\mathrm{M}$  image-text pairs.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions training on 'internal datasets' with 460M pairs and LAION-400M with 400M pairs, totaling ~860M pairs. However, it's unclear whether these datasets overlap, how the internal datasets were curated, or what proportion of training came from each source. This matters for reproducibility and understanding potential biases. Add clarification: 'The internal datasets consist of [description] and do not overlap with LAION-400M. Training used [X]% internal data and [Y]% LAION-400M data per epoch.'

comments.title: Inconsistent guidance weight values
comments.quote: Table 1: MS-COCO  $256 \times 256$  FID-30K. We use a guidance weight of 1.35 for our  $64 \times 64$  model, and a guidance weight of 8.0 for our super-resolution model.
comments.explanation: The guidance weight values appear inconsistent with typical classifier-free guidance practices. The 64×64 base model uses guidance weight 1.35 while super-resolution uses 8.0. Since guidance weights typically range from 1-10 and higher values increase adherence to text conditioning, this discrepancy needs clarification. The paper should specify whether these values are used during training, inference, or both, and explain the rationale for different weights across model stages. Add clarification after the quoted text: 'The guidance weight of 1.35 is used during training of the base model, while 8.0 is used during inference for super-resolution models.'

comments.title: Duplicate paragraph in introduction
comments.quote: $256\times 256$ and $1024\times 1024$ images (see Fig. A.4). All diffusion models are conditioned on the text embedding sequence and use classifier-free guidance *[27]*. Imagen relies on new sampling techniques to allow usage of large guidance weights without sample quality degradation observed in prior work, resulting in images with higher fidelity and better image-text alignment than previously possible.
comments.explanation: This paragraph appears twice in the introduction section - once on page 1 and again after Figure 1. The duplication is a clear editorial error that disrupts the flow of the introduction. Remove the second occurrence of this paragraph after Figure 1 to eliminate the redundancy.

comments.title: Inconsistent reference formatting
comments.quote: Imagen comprises a frozen T5-XXL [54] encoder to map input text into a sequence of embeddings and a $64 \times 64$ image diffusion model, followed by two super-resolution diffusion models for generating $256 \times 256$ and $1024 \times 1024$ images (see Fig. A.4). All diffusion models are conditioned on the text embedding sequence and use classifier-free guidance [27].
comments.explanation: The reference formatting is inconsistent within the same paragraph. T5-XXL uses brackets [54] while classifier-free guidance uses brackets *[27]* with asterisks. Later in the same paragraph, classifier-free guidance is cited as *[27]* again. This inconsistency should be standardized - either use [27] throughout or *[27]* throughout, but not both formats in the same paragraph.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about prior work training data
comments.quote: In contrast to prior work that uses only image-text data for model training [e.g., 55, 43], the key finding behind Imagen is that text embeddings from large LMs [54, 15], pretrained on text-only corpora, are remarkably effective for text-to-image synthesis.
comments.explanation: This claim mischaracterizes prior work. GLIDE [43] actually uses CLIP text embeddings, where CLIP was trained on image-text pairs, but the text encoder component can leverage text-only pretraining patterns. More importantly, DALL-E 2 [56] (cited later as concurrent work) also uses CLIP embeddings in a similar way. The contrast should be more precisely stated as 'prior work that jointly trains text and image encoders on image-text data' rather than suggesting they don't leverage text-only pretraining at all.

comments.title: Missing component definition in overview
comments.quote: Imagen consists of a text encoder that maps text to a sequence of embeddings and a cascade of conditional diffusion models that map these embeddings to images of increasing resolutions (see Fig. A.4). In the following subsections, we describe each of these components in detail.
comments.explanation: The overview introduces two main components (text encoder and cascade of diffusion models) but doesn't specify how many diffusion models are in the cascade or what the target resolutions are. This creates ambiguity about the architecture scope. The paper should state the number of diffusion models and their resolution targets (e.g., 64×64, 256×256, 1024×1024) in this overview to match the detailed descriptions that follow.

comments.title: Incomplete sentence about T5-XXL and CLIP
comments.quote:
We also find that while T5-XXL and CLIP

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

text encoders perform similarly on simple benchmarks such as MS-COCO, human evaluators prefer T5-XXL encoders over CLIP text encoders in both image-text alignment and image fidelity on DrawBench, a set of challenging and compositional prompts.
comments.explanation: The sentence beginning with 'We also find that while T5-XXL and CLIP' is split across a page break, creating a grammatical fragment. The text 'text encoders perform similarly' appears to be missing from the first part of the sentence. This should be reformatted to ensure the complete sentence is readable without the page break disrupting the flow. The sentence should read as a complete thought: 'We also find that while T5-XXL and CLIP text encoders perform similarly on simple benchmarks such as MS-COCO, human evaluators prefer T5-XXL encoders over CLIP text encoders...'

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in classifier-free guidance equation
comments.quote:
Sampling is performed using the adjusted $\mathbf{x}$-prediction $(\mathbf{z}_{t}-\sigma\tilde{\bm{\epsilon}}_{\theta})/\alpha_{t}$, where

$\tilde{\bm{\epsilon}}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t},\mathbf{c})=w\bm{\epsilon}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t},\mathbf{c})+(1-w)\bm{\epsilon}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t})$ (2)

Here, $\bm{\epsilon}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t},\mathbf{c})$ and $\bm{\epsilon}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t})$ are conditional and unconditional $\bm{\epsilon}$-predictions, given by $\bm{\epsilon}_{\theta}\coloneqq(\mathbf{z}_{t}-\alpha_{t}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta})/\sigma_{t}$,
comments.explanation: In the adjusted prediction formula $(\mathbf{z}_{t}-\sigma\tilde{\bm{\epsilon}}_{\theta})/\alpha_{t}$, the term $\sigma$ lacks the time subscript $t$, while the $\epsilon$-prediction definition uses $\sigma_t$. Since equation (1) establishes $\sigma_t$ as a time-dependent function and the diffusion process is inherently time-varying, $\sigma$ should indeed be $\sigma_t$ for consistency. Rewrite the adjusted prediction as $(\mathbf{z}_{t}-\sigma_t\tilde{\bm{\epsilon}}_{\theta})/\alpha_{t}$ to maintain consistency with the time-dependent noise schedule notation established in equation (1).

comments.title: Undefined notation in adjusted prediction formula
comments.quote: Sampling is performed using the adjusted $\mathbf{x}$-prediction $(\mathbf{z}_{t}-\sigma\tilde{\bm{\epsilon}}_{\theta})/\alpha_{t}$, where
comments.explanation:
The adjusted prediction formula introduces $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}$ notation without definition. While $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}$ is defined in equation (1) as the denoising model, $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}$ appears only in the $\epsilon$-prediction definition. The relationship between $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}$ is unclear - they may be identical or $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}$ might represent the $\mathbf{x}_0$ prediction at time $t$.

Add a clarification after equation (2): 'Here $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t}, \mathbf{c})$ represents the model's prediction of the original data $\mathbf{x}$ from the noisy input $\mathbf{z}_{t}$.'

comments.title: Missing guidance weight threshold analysis
comments.quote: We find that dynamic thresholding results in significantly better photorealism as well as better image-text alignment, especially when using very large guidance weights.
comments.explanation: The claim about dynamic thresholding's effectiveness with 'very large guidance weights' lacks quantitative specification. The section does not define what constitutes 'very large' guidance weights, nor does it provide the range of weights tested. Without this information, readers cannot determine the practical limits of the technique or reproduce the results. Add specific guidance weight values (e.g., 'for weights > 15') and include the range tested to make the claim verifiable.

comments.title: Incomplete noise conditioning augmentation description
comments.quote: we corrupt the low-resolution image with the augmentation (corresponding to $\mathrm{aug\_level}$), and condition the diffusion model on $\mathrm{aug\_level}$. During training, $\mathrm{aug\_level}$ is chosen randomly, while during inference, we sweep over its different values to find the best sample quality.
